content:
# Leviticus Chapter 24 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 72v–73r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_024_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_024_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 110–111 | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Command the sons of Israel that they bring to you pure beaten olive oil for the light, to cause a lamp to burn continually.

**3** Outside the veil of the testimony, in the tent of meeting, Aaron shall arrange it from evening to morning before the LORD continually. It shall be a statute forever throughout your generations.

**4** He shall arrange the lamps on the pure gold lampstand before the LORD continually.

**5** "And you shall take fine flour and bake twelve loaves from it; two tenths of an ephah shall be in each loaf.

**6** And you shall set them in two rows, six in a row, on the pure gold table before the LORD.

**7** And you shall put pure frankincense on each row, that it may go with the bread as a memorial portion, a food offering to the LORD.

**8** Every Sabbath day he shall arrange it before the LORD continually; it is from the sons of Israel as a covenant forever.

**9** And it shall be for Aaron and his sons, and they shall eat it in a holy place, for it is most holy to him from the LORD's food offerings — a perpetual due."

**10** Now a man who was the son of an Israelite woman went out — and his father was an Egyptian man — among the sons of Israel. And the son of the Israelite woman and a man of Israel quarreled in the camp.

**11** And the son of the Israelite woman blasphemed the Name and cursed. So they brought him to Moses. His mother's name was Shelomith, the daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan.

**12** And they put him in custody, until the will of the LORD should be plain to them.

**13** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**14** "Bring the one who has cursed outside the camp, and let all who heard him lay their hands on his head, and let all the congregation stone him.

**15** And you shall speak to the sons of Israel, saying: Whoever curses his God shall bear his sin.

**16** Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD shall surely be put to death. All the congregation shall stone him. The sojourner as well as the native, when he blasphemes the Name, shall be put to death.

**17** "Whoever takes a human life shall surely be put to death.

**18** Whoever takes an animal's life shall make it good, life for life.

**19** If anyone injures his neighbor, as he has done it shall be done to him:

**20** fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth. Whatever injury he has given a person shall be given to him.

**21** Whoever kills an animal shall make it good, and whoever kills a person shall be put to death.

**22** You shall have one law for the sojourner and for the native, for I am the LORD your God."

**23** And Moses spoke to the sons of Israel, and they brought the one who had cursed outside the camp and stoned him with stones. And the sons of Israel did as the LORD had commanded Moses.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Pure beaten olive oil" (v. 2):** Hebrew שֶׁמֶן זַיִת זָךְ כָּתִית. The adjective זָךְ ("pure, clear") and the passive participle כָּתִית ("beaten, crushed") both appear. The oil was produced by crushing olives in a mortar rather than pressing, yielding a finer, cleaner oil with less sediment — appropriate for the sanctuary lamp. The LXX reads ἔλαιον ἐλάϊνον καθαρὸν κεκομμένον, confirming both qualifiers.

2. **"To cause a lamp to burn continually" (v. 2):** Hebrew לְהַעֲלֹת נֵר תָּמִיד. The infinitive הַעֲלֹת from עלה in the hiphil means "to cause to go up" — a technical term for lighting or maintaining a lamp so its flame rises. "Continually" (תָּמִיד) is a key cultic adverb throughout this chapter (vv. 2, 3, 4, 8). The LXX καίειν λύχνον διαπαντός matches.

3. **"Outside the veil of the testimony" (v. 3):** Hebrew מִחוּץ לְפָרֹכֶת הָעֵדֻת. The parochet (פָּרֹכֶת) is the heavy curtain separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place. "The testimony" (הָעֵדֻת) refers to the tablets of the law housed in the ark. The menorah was kept in the outer chamber (Holy Place), lit each evening and tended each morning. The LXX renders ἔξω τοῦ καταπετάσματος τοῦ μαρτυρίου.

4. **"Aaron shall arrange it from evening to morning" (v. 3):** Hebrew יַעֲרֹךְ אֹתוֹ אַהֲרֹן מֵעֶרֶב עַד-בֹּקֶר. The verb עָרַךְ ("arrange, set in order") is the same verb used for arranging battle lines and for setting the wood on the altar — a technical term implying careful, ordered preparation. This ritual required tending the lamps each evening and morning (cf. Exod 27:21; 30:7–8).

5. **"Pure gold lampstand" (v. 4):** Hebrew מְנֹרַת הַזָּהָב הַטָּהוֹר. The menorah (מְנֹרָה), the seven-branched lampstand described in Exod 25:31–40, is here specified as made of pure gold (הַזָּהָב הַטָּהוֹר). The LXX confirms ἐπὶ τῆς λυχνίας τῆς καθαρᾶς. LOGOS renders "lampstand" for מְנֹרָה throughout to maintain formal equivalence with the Hebrew construct.

6. **"Two tenths of an ephah" (v. 5):** Hebrew שְׁנֵי עֶשְׂרֹנִים. An ephah is roughly 22 liters; a tenth (עִשָּׂרוֹן) is approximately 2.2 liters. Each loaf therefore contained about 4.4 liters of flour — a substantial loaf. The same measure appears in Num 28:9 for the Sabbath offering.

7. **"Bread of the Presence" / "showbread" (vv. 5–9):** The Hebrew לֶחֶם הַפָּנִים does not appear explicitly in this passage; the text speaks simply of "loaves" (חַלּוֹת) set "before the LORD" (לִפְנֵי יְהוָה). This is the same bread called לֶחֶם הַפָּנִים ("bread of the Presence") in other passages (Exod 25:30; 1 Sam 21:6). LOGOS here follows the passage's own language: "before the LORD."

8. **"Pure frankincense" (v. 7):** Hebrew לְבֹנָה זַכָּה. The adjective זַכָּה ("pure, clear") qualifies the frankincense (לְבֹנָה). This resin was placed in bowls alongside the bread rows and burnt as the "memorial portion" (אַזְכָּרָה) — the portion that ascends to God in fire — while the bread itself was consumed by the priests (v. 9). The LXX reads λίβανον καθαρόν.

9. **"Memorial portion" (v. 7):** Hebrew אַזְכָּרָה, from זָכַר ("to remember"). This technical cultic term refers to the token portion of an offering burnt on the altar as a representative act, causing God to "remember" the offerer. It appears at grain offerings (Lev 2:2, 9, 16) and here at the showbread. The LXX renders εἰς ἀνάμνησιν, "for remembrance."

10. **"As a covenant forever" (v. 8):** Hebrew בְּרִית עוֹלָם. The showbread arrangement is designated a perpetual covenant — not merely a statute (חֻקָּה) but a covenantal obligation binding Israel to the LORD. This elevates the rite to the level of the Noahic and Abrahamic covenants in Levitical theology.

11. **"The son of an Israelite woman" / "an Egyptian man" (v. 10):** The narrative carefully specifies the man's mixed parentage: his mother is Israelite, his father Egyptian. This is the sole narrative interlude in the Holiness Code's final chapters. The man's identity is ambiguous in law — does he belong among the "sons of Israel"? The narrative does not resolve this before stating the verdict, leaving the legal tension visible.

12. **"Blasphemed the Name and cursed" (v. 11):** Hebrew וַיִּקֹּב בֶּן-הָאִשָּׁה הַיִּשְׂרְאֵלִית אֶת-הַשֵּׁם וַיְקַלֵּל. The verb נָקַב (Qal) means "to pierce, puncture" — here used euphemistically and intensively: to "pierce through" the Name, i.e., to pronounce it with blasphemous intent. The second verb קָלַל (Pi'el) means "to curse, to treat as light or contemptible." Both verbs appear; "blasphemed" renders נָקַב and "cursed" renders קִלֵּל, preserving the dual action. The LXX uses ἐπονομάσας ("having named/called upon") and κατηράσατο ("cursed").

13. **"The Name" (vv. 11, 16):** Hebrew הַשֵּׁם with the definite article — "the Name" — is a reverential circumlocution for the divine name YHWH. In v. 16 the expanded phrase "the name of the LORD" (שֵׁם יְהוָה) appears alongside the shorter "the Name" (הַשֵּׁם), giving the translator "the name of the LORD" and "the Name" as distinct renderings. LOGOS preserves this distinction.

14. **"Shelomith, the daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan" (v. 11):** The naming of the mother — and only the mother — is narratively significant. Mothers are rarely named in legal narratives; the father is identified only by ethnicity (Egyptian). Shelomith's name (from שָׁלוֹם, "peace") is ironic given her son's violent offense. Dibri (דִּבְרִי) may be related to דָּבַר, "word/speech" — a further irony in a blasphemy narrative.

15. **"Until the will of the LORD should be plain to them" (v. 12):** Hebrew לִפְרֹשׁ לָהֶם עַל-פִּי יְהוָה. The verb פָּרַשׁ means "to make distinct, to explain, to clarify." The phrase "al-pi YHWH" ("according to the mouth of the LORD") indicates that Mosaic adjudication required direct divine revelation. The LXX renders διαστεῖλαι αὐτοῖς διὰ προστάγματος κυρίου, "to distinguish/decide for them by the command of the LORD."

16. **"Let all who heard him lay their hands on his head" (v. 14):** The witnesses place their hands (סְמִיכָה) on the blasphemer's head before the stoning. This gesture transfers culpability back to the offender and exonerates the community from bloodguilt. The gesture mirrors the hand-laying at sacrificial offerings (Lev 1:4; 3:2; 4:4) but in reverse — marking the condemned rather than the offering.

17. **"Whoever curses his God shall bear his sin" (v. 15):** Hebrew וְאִישׁ כִּי יְקַלֵּל אֱלֹהָיו וְנָשָׂא חֶטְאוֹ. The subject is "a man" (אִישׁ) who curses "his God" (אֱלֹהָיו) — notably using the generic Elohim rather than YHWH. The verb נָשָׂא ("bear, carry") with חֵטְא ("sin") indicates personal liability for the offense. The LXX reads ἄνθρωπος ὃς ἐὰν καταράσηται θεόν, ἁμαρτίαν λήμψεται.

18. **"Blasphemes the name of the LORD shall surely be put to death" (v. 16):** Hebrew וְנֹקֵב שֵׁם-יְהוָה מוֹת יוּמָת. The Qal active participle of נָקַב governs this capital verdict. The infinitive absolute construction מוֹת יוּמָת (lit. "dying he shall die") is rendered "shall surely be put to death" — the standard LOGOS rendering of this emphatic Hebrew idiom. The penalty applies equally to sojourner and native (גֵּר וְאֶזְרָח, vv. 16, 22).

19. **Lex talionis (vv. 17–21):** The principle of proportional restitution (lit. "an eye for an eye") appears here in three paired statements:
    - Human life: capital punishment (v. 17)
    - Animal life: replacement in kind (v. 18)
    - Human bodily injury: equivalent injury (vv. 19–20)
    Then repeated in reverse order (vv. 21–22). The formula "fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth" (שֶׁבֶר תַּחַת שֶׁבֶר עַיִן תַּחַת עַיִן שֵׁן תַּחַת שֵׁן) renders תַּחַת ("under, in place of") with "for." The lex talionis is not vindictive escalation but legal proportionality — a ceiling on retaliation, not a floor. The LXX σύντριμμα ἀντὶ συντρίμματος, ὀφθαλμὸν ἀντὶ ὀφθαλμοῦ, ὀδόντα ἀντὶ ὀδόντος closely matches.

20. **"Whatever injury he has given a person shall be given to him" (v. 20):** Hebrew כַּאֲשֶׁר יִתֵּן מוּם בָּאָדָם כֵּן יִנָּתֶן בּוֹ. The term מוּם ("blemish, defect, disfigurement") is the same word used for disqualifying defects in priests (Lev 21:17–23) and animals (Lev 22:20–25). Its use here for bodily injury to persons imports the priestly vocabulary of wholeness and impairment into the criminal law.

21. **"Sojourner" (vv. 16, 22):** Hebrew גֵּר, the resident alien or immigrant living within the Israelite community. The LXX renders προσήλυτος, "proselyte/convert" — a narrowing that reflects later Jewish practice but may not fully represent the broader Mosaic category. LOGOS renders גֵּר as "sojourner" throughout to avoid importing that later distinction.

22. **"I am the LORD your God" (v. 22):** The chapter closes with the standard Holiness Code formula (אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם), grounding the legal equality of sojourner and native in the character of God rather than in social contract. This formula recurs as the ultimate warrant for all Holiness legislation (cf. Lev 18:2; 19:2; 20:7, 24, 26; 25:17, 38, 55; 26:1, 13, 44–45).

23. **LXX Significant Variants:**
    - V. 7: LXX adds "and you shall place on each row salt" — a plus not in the MT, possibly harmonizing with the salt requirement for grain offerings (Lev 2:13).
    - V. 11: LXX ἐπονομάσας (Vaticanus) for נָקַב — "to name/call upon" rather than "to pierce/blaspheme" — may soften the force or reflect a different understanding of the act.
    - V. 16: LXX omits the repeated phrase "when he blasphemes the Name, shall be put to death" at the end of the verse, making the death sentence conditional only on the act of "naming" (ὀνομάζων), whereas the MT has a double verdict — once for blaspheming "the name of the LORD" and once for blaspheming "the Name" (the short form). The MT's double formulation appears intentional: it encompasses both the fuller and shorter forms of the blasphemous utterance.

24. **Conjunctive "And" (throughout):** The Hebrew waw-consecutive (וַיְדַבֵּר, וַיֵּצֵא, etc.) is consistently rendered "And" at the start of verses and clauses, preserving the Hebrew narrative connective rather than smoothing into English prose.
